inputs.story: This year's All-Star Hockey Team has a number of top players from all across the national league. Match each player to his team and jersey number, and determine how many goals he has scored this past season. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.
2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.
3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.
4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.
5. Ingram is either the player from the Checkers or the player with 6 goals.
inputs.constants:
players: Ingram; Parrish; Quinn; Underwood.
goals: 6; 7; 8; 9.
teams: Checkers; Comets; Ice Hogs; Monsters.
inputs.solution:
ingram	7	checkers
underwood	6	comets
parrish	9	ice hogs
quinn	8	monsters

constants_formatting.prompt:
Given categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", format the category and constants such that:
each category consists of only lowercase letters and underscores, and
each constant is either an integer or a string surrounded by double quotes, e.g., "United States".

There are two ways below to format constants and we must use the same way for all constants of the same category.
1. Turn all constants of the same category into integers with no space or special character.
2. Add double quotes around all constants of the same category.
Note that the 1st way has a higher priority, meaning that we must turn all constants of the same category into integers whenever possible. For example, twice or second can be turned into 2, September can be turned into 9, September 5th can be turned into 5 if all dates are in September, but 9:30am can only be turned into "9:30am" since no integer can represent 9:30am.

Original constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood typse: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

Formatted constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Original constants:
months: January; April; October; December.
times: 8:30AM; 10:30AM; 2:30PM; 3:30PM.
durations: 1 day; 3 days; 11 days; 12 days.

Formatted constants:
months: 1; 4; 10; 12.
time: "8:30AM"; "10:30PM"; "2:30PM"; "3:30PM".
duration: 1; 3; 11; 12.

Original constants:
players: Ingram; Parrish; Quinn; Underwood.
goals: 6; 7; 8; 9.
teams: Checkers; Comets; Ice Hogs; Monsters.

Formatted constants:

predicates.prompt:
Given a problem and some categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", generate the minimum number of predicates to define the relations among the categories of constants. Each generated predicate is of the form "predicate(X1, X2, ..., Xn)" where X1, X2, ..., Xn are different variables and each variable X belongs to one of the categories. For each category, there must exist at least one variable of some predicate that belongs to this category.

Problem 1:
Consider N-Queens Puzzle on a chessboard of size 8x8. The goal is to assign 8 queens on the chessboard so that no two queens can share the same row, column, or diagonal.

Constants:
index_of_row: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.
index_of_column: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include index_of_row and index_of_column. We use different variables Ir and Ic to represent index_of_row and index_of_column.
% We assign a queen at row Ir and column Ic, where Ir belongs to index_of_row and Ic belongs to index_of_column.
assign(Ir, Ic)

Problem 2:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Problem 3:
This year's All-Star Hockey Team has a number of top players from all across the national league. Match each player to his team and jersey number, and determine how many goals he has scored this past season. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.
2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.
3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.
4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.
5. Ingram is either the player from the Checkers or the player with 6 goals.

Constants:
players: "Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood".  
goals: 6; 7; 8; 9.  
teams: "Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters".

Predicates:
predicates.response:
We are tasked with generating the **minimum number of predicates** to define the relations among the categories of constants in **Problem 3**, following the same pattern as in Problems 1 and 2.

---

### **Problem 3 Recap:**

**Categories:**
- `players`: "Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood"
- `goals`: 6; 7; 8; 9
- `teams`: "Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters"

Each player is associated with:
- One team
- One jersey number (not explicitly given — but wait, **jersey number is not a category** in the constants)

Wait — let's re-read carefully:

> "Match each player to his team and jersey number, and determine how many goals he has scored this past season."

But in the **constants**, we only have:
- `players`: "Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood"
- `goals`: 6; 7; 8; 9
- `teams`: "Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters"

**There is no mention of jersey numbers in the constants.**

But the problem says: "Match each player to his team and jersey number" — so **jersey number** must be a missing category.

However, **no values are given for jersey numbers** in the constants.

This is a problem.

But looking back at the **format of the constants** in previous problems:

- Problem 1: `index_of_row`, `index_of_column` — both with 8 values
- Problem 2: `employee`, `price`, `wood_type` — each with 3 values
- Problem 3: `players`, `goals`, `teams` — 4 values each

So we expect **one category per variable** in the predicate.

But the problem mentions **jersey number** as part of the task, yet it's **not included in the constants**.

This suggests a **missing category**.

But the instruction says: *"Given a problem and some categorized constants..."*, so we must work **only with the provided constants**.

Therefore, **even though the problem mentions jersey number**, since it's **not listed in the constants**, we **cannot** include it in our predicates.

So we must conclude that **"jersey number" is not part of the formal logic model**, or perhaps it's a red herring.

But wait — the problem says: *"Match each player to his team and jersey number..."*, so **we are expected to assign a jersey number**.

But no values are given. So unless we are told what the jersey numbers are, we cannot define a category.

Thus, the only logical conclusion is that **"jersey number" is not a formal category in this logic puzzle**, or it's implied that we don't need to model it — but that contradicts the problem statement.

Alternatively, perhaps **"jersey number" is not a category to be modeled**, and the puzzle only requires us to match players to teams and goals.

But the problem says: "Match each player to his team and jersey number", which implies **three attributes per player**.

But we only have **three categories**: players, goals, teams.

So unless **jersey number is not a variable**, we are missing a category.

Wait — perhaps **"jersey number" is not a separate category**, but rather **implied by the structure**? Or maybe it's a mistake?

Let’s re-express the problem:

> "Match each player to his team and jersey number, and determine how many goals he has scored this past season."

So the attributes are:
- Team
- Jersey number
- Goals

But **only goals and teams** are in the constants.

So **either**:
1. The puzzle is incomplete (missing jersey number constants), or
2. The **jersey number is not to be modeled**, or
3. The **jersey number is implicitly defined** (e.g., by the player or team), but we are not given values.

Given that in **Problem 2**, we had three categories and used a ternary predicate `match(E, P, W)` — and the puzzle only required matching employee, price, and wood type — we can assume that **only the categories in the constants are to be modeled**.

Thus, even though the problem says "match to team and jersey number", **if jersey number is not in the constants**, we **cannot** include it.

Therefore, we must **ignore jersey number** for the purpose of predicate generation.

So we are to **model the relation between players, teams, and goals**.

But wait — **each player has one team, one goal count**, and we are to assign them.

So we need a predicate that relates:
- A player
- A team
- A goal count

So the predicate should be:

```prolog
player_info(Player, Team, Goals)
```

But the format in previous problems was:

> `match(E, P, W)` — where E, P, W are variables from the categories.

So we can define:

```prolog
player_info(P, T, G)
```

where:
- P ∈ players
- T ∈ teams
- G ∈ goals

And this captures the full assignment.

Now, the requirement is: **generate the minimum number of predicates** to define the relations among the categories.

We have **three categories**: players, teams, goals.

We need to relate them.

We can do this with **one predicate** that connects one variable from each category.

Is it possible to use **more than one predicate**? Yes, but we want the **minimum number**.

Can we do it with **one predicate**?

Yes — if we define a single predicate that links each player to a team and a goal count.

This is standard in logic puzzles: one ternary predicate for the full assignment.

Moreover, the problem says: "Each generated predicate is of the form `predicate(X1, X2, ..., Xn)` where X1, X2, ..., Xn are different variables and each variable X belongs to one of the categories."

So we can have:

```prolog
player_info(P, T, G)
```

This uses:
- P from `players`
- T from `teams`
- G from `goals`

And each category is represented.

Can we do it with **fewer than one**? No.

So **minimum number is 1**.

But wait — could we use **two predicates**?

For example:
- `plays(P, T)` — player to team
- `scores(P, G)` — player to goals

But then we’d need **two predicates**, and the problem asks for **minimum number**.

So **one is better**.

But is one sufficient?

Yes — because the ternary predicate `player_info(P, T, G)` captures the full assignment: each player is assigned a team and a goal count.

And since no option is repeated (as per the puzzle rule), this is a bijection.

Thus, one predicate is sufficient and minimal.

But wait — in **Problem 2

search_space.prompt:
Given some categorized constants in the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n" and some predicates about the relation among different categories of constants, write ASP (Answer Set Programming) rules to generate the search space of possible relations. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the rules - it will be automatically parsed from your response.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

ASP Rules:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% For each employee E, it matches with exactly 1 price P and 1 wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).
```

Constants:
players: "Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood".  
goals: 6; 7; 8; 9.  
teams: "Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters".

Predicates:
player_info(Player, Team, Goals)

ASP Rules:
search_space.response:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood").
goals(6; 7; 8; 9).
teams("Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters").

% Each player is assigned to exactly one team and has exactly one goal count.
{player_info(Player, Team, Goals): teams(Team), goals(Goals)}=1 :- players(Player).
```
search_space.extracted:
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood").
goals(6; 7; 8; 9).
teams("Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters").

% Each player is assigned to exactly one team and has exactly one goal count.
{player_info(Player, Team, Goals): teams(Team), goals(Goals)}=1 :- players(Player).

paraphrasing.prompt:
Copy a sequence of numbered sentences.

If a sentence is of the form "N. Of A and B, one is C and the other is D", replace it with 3 sentences below.
N.1 A and B are different.
N.2 A is C or D.
N.3 B is C or D.

For every sentence, if it is not of the form "N. Of ... and ...", simply copy it without replacement. An easy way to determine if a sentence is not of the above form is to check if its first word is not of.

In the following example, one sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2. Of the team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals, one finished with 2 gold medals and the other finished with 1 gold medal.
Copy:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2.1 The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
2.2 The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
2.3 The team that won 10 silver medals finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.

In the following example, no sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
Copy:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Given:
1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.
2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.
3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.
4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.
5. Ingram is either the player from the Checkers or the player with 6 goals.
Copy:

2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.  
3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.  
4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.  
5.1 Ingram and the player with 6 goals are different.  
5.2 Ingram is the player from the Checkers or Ingram is the player with 6 goals.

prompt.content: You are a semantic parser to turn clues in a problem into logical rules using only provided constants and predicates.

Consider the constraint in the following form
<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.
which says that if the conjunction "<L1> and <L2> and ... and <Ln>" is true, then the disjunction of comparisons "<C1> or <C2> or ... or <Cm>" must be true.

One can also add a restriction that "exactly k of <C1>, <C2>, ..., <Cm> is true" by using the following form
{<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.

Given a problem, extract all constraints from the clues in the problem using only the provided constants and predicates. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the constraints - it will be automatically parsed from your response.
Ok. I will only write constraints under the provided forms.

Problem 1:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.
```

Problem 2:
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold, silver and bronze medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
country: "Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman".
silver_medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
gold_medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

Predicates:
% The categories include country, silver_medals, and gold_medals. We use different variables C, S, and G to represent country, silver_medals, and gold_medals.
% We assign a country C with silver medals S and gold medals G, where C belongs to country, S belongs to silver_medals, and G belongs to gold_medals.
assign(C, S, G)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.
```

Problem 3:
This year's All-Star Hockey Team has a number of top players from all across the national league. Match each player to his team and jersey number, and determine how many goals he has scored this past season. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.  
2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.  
3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.  
4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.  
5.1 Ingram and the player with 6 goals are different.  
5.2 Ingram is the player from the Checkers or Ingram is the player with 6 goals.

Constants:
players: "Ingram"; "Parrish"; "Quinn"; "Underwood".  
goals: 6; 7; 8; 9.  
teams: "Checkers"; "Comets"; "Ice Hogs"; "Monsters".

Predicates:
player_info(Player, Team, Goals)

Constraints:

constraints.response:
```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{P1=P2; T1=T2; G1=G2}=0 :- player_info(P1,T1,G1), player_info(P2,T2,G2), (P1,T1,G1)!=(P2,T2,G2).

% 1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.
G=7 :- player_info(P,T,G), P="Ingram".

% 2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.
{P="Underwood"; T="Ice Hogs"; T="Monsters"; T="Checkers"}=1 :- player_info(P,T,G).

% 3. Parrish scored 1 goal more than Quinn.
G1=G2+1 :- player_info(P1,T1,G1), player_info(P2,T2,G2), P1="Parrish", P2="Quinn".

% 4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.
T="Ice Hogs" :- player_info(P,T,G), G=9.

% 5.1 Ingram and the player with 6 goals are different.
P1!=P2 :- player_info(P1,T1,G1), player_info(P2,T2,G2), P1="Ingram", G2=6.

% 5.2 Ingram is the player from the Checkers or Ingram is the player with 6 goals.
{T="Checkers"; G=6}=1 :- player_info(P,T,G), P="Ingram".
```

% 1. Ingram had 7 goals this season.

% 2. The four players are Underwood, the player from the Ice Hogs, the player from the Monsters and the player from the Checkers.

{T="Checkers"; G=6}=1 :- player_info(P,T,G), P="Ingram".

G1=G2+1 :- player_info(P1,T1,G1), player_info(P2,T2,G2), P1="Parrish", P2="Quinn".

% 4. The player with 9 goals is from the Ice Hogs.
T="Ice Hogs" :- player_info(P,T,G), G=9.

% 5.1 Ingram and the player with 6 goals are different.
P1!=P2 :- player_info(P1,T1,G1), player_info(P2,T2,G2), P1="Ingram", G2=6.

% 5.2 Ingram is the player from the Checkers or Ingram is the player with 6 goals.
{T="Checkers"; G=6}=1 :- player_info(P,T,G), P="Ingram".

final.ground_truth:
ingram	7	checkers
underwood	6	comets
parrish	9	ice hogs
